verses.text: A seção maior ( **13.1-17.27** ) conclui com a profecia de Natã sobre Davi e a oração de agradecimento de Davi.\n\n

A seção maior ( **13.1-17.27** ) conclui com a profecia de Natã sobre Davi e a oração de agradecimento de Davi.\n\n

Davi havia decidido construir uma casa para o Senhor — o Templo. Em vez disso, o Senhor construiria uma casa para Davi — uma dinastia de reis. Estabelecer a dinastia eterna de Davi era a parte central do plano de Deus para estabelecer Seu governo na terra.\n\n

Davi havia decidido construir uma casa para o Senhor — o Templo. Em vez disso, o Senhor construiria uma casa para Davi — uma dinastia de reis. Estabelecer a dinastia eterna de Davi era a parte central do plano de Deus para estabelecer Seu governo na terra.\n\n
A construção do Templo foi um aspecto central do reino de Deus na terra. Salomão mais tarde construiu o Templo ( **2Cr 2-7** ) e cumpriu essa promessa.
O Cronista fez esta declaração sobre Salomão em particular (veja **22.9** ; **28.5-6** ; cp. **2Sm 7.14** ).

No paralelo em **2Sm 7.16** , Deus promete estabelecer a casa, o reino e o trono de Davi, enquanto aqui é o de Salomão. Enquanto os livros de Samuel e Reis retratam Davi como o rei ideal de Israel, o Cronista vê Salomão como igual a Davi. Davi fez todos os preparativos para a construção do Templo, mas Deus designou Salomão como o construtor do Templo. A promessa a Israel foi assegurada nos reinados combinados de Davi e Salomão.
17:15 According to all . . . this vision—The revelation of the divine will was made to the prophet in a dream.
17:16 And what is mine house, that thou hast brought me hitherto? - I am not of any regal family, and have no natural right to the throne.

17:17 This was a small thing in thine eyes, O God: David marvels that God has not only granted the present favor of dynasty but spoken of the family's distant future. The phrase *lemerakhok* (for a great while to come) opens onto a horizon beyond David's vision — ultimately fulfilled in the Messiah (Luke 1:32-33).

17:18 What can David speak more to thee for the honour of thy servant?: The king's prayer reaches the limits of speech. *Lekavod* (for the honor) signals that God's gift exceeds any return David can offer — exposing the asymmetry of grace and gratitude that runs through the whole prayer.

17:19 For thy servant's sake, and according to thine own heart: David grounds the divine kindness not in his merit but in God's character — *kelibekha* (according to thine own heart). The promise to David's house springs from the same source as the call to Abraham: God's freely chosen love (cp. Deut 7:7-8; Eph 1:5).

17:20 There is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee: David's doxology echoes the Shema (Deut 6:4) and Hannah's prayer (1 Sam 2:2). The recital of God's incomparability sets the dynastic promise within the larger story of redemption — a covenant kept by the one God of all the earth.

17:21 What one nation in the earth is like thy people Israel: The promise to David is rooted in the prior covenant with the people. *Ge'ulah* — redemption from Egypt — established Israel as God's own, and the Davidic gift extends that founding act forward (cp. 2 Sam 7:23; Titus 2:14).

17:22 Thy people Israel didst thou make thine own people for ever: David seals the previous verse with the formula of covenant possession. The Lord's *am* (people) and Israel's *Elohim* (God) form the irreducible covenant pair (cp. Exod 6:7; Jer 31:33; Rev 21:3) that ultimately reaches its fulfillment in the new covenant.

17:23 Let the thing that thou hast spoken concerning thy servant be established: David's prayer turns from praise to petition — that God would do as he has promised. *Yeaman* (let it be established) borrows the language of treaty-sealing. David asks for nothing the Lord has not already pledged.

17:24 Let it even be established, that thy name may be magnified for ever: The motive of David's petition is the glory of God's name, not personal grandeur. The phrase *yigdal shimkha* shapes a model of kingly prayer — interest tied to God's reputation more than dynastic perpetuity (cp. John 12:28; 17:1).
17:25 Hath found in his heart to pray - The Targum expresses a full sense: "Therefore thy servant hath found an opening of mouth, that he might pray before Thee.

17:26 Now, Lord, thou art God, and hast promised this goodness: The prayer's closing affirmation. David names the promise simply *hatovah* — the good thing — and rests his hope on God's own self-binding word. The dynasty would survive coups, exile, and silence on the same foundation (cp. 2 Sam 7:28; Heb 6:17-18).

17:27 For thou blessest, O Lord - "Thou beginnest to bless the house of thy servant, therefore it shall be blessed for ever." - T. The reader is requested to refer to 2 Samuel 7 (note), and the notes there for many particulars that belong to the parallel places here, and which it should answer no good purpose to repeat in this place.
